bm25: BM25 is a ranking function for search. It scores a document by how often the query terms appear, but with two corrections plain TF-IDF lacks: term-frequency saturation (the k1 parameter, so a word repeated many times stops helping) and document-length normalization (the b parameter, so long documents are not unfairly favored).
rag: Retrieval-Augmented Generation puts a retrieval step in front of a language model: fetch relevant documents, then ground the model's answer in them. The hard part is not generation but knowing whether the answer is actually supported by what was retrieved.
mcp: The Model Context Protocol is a JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol that lets a language-model client discover and call tools exposed by a server. A local server speaks it over stdio: the client sends initialize, the server replies with its capabilities, then tools/list enumerates tools and tools/call invokes one.
ndcg: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain measures ranked-retrieval quality. Unlike precision or recall at k, it is rank-aware: a relevant document at position 1 scores higher than the same document at position 10, because each hit is discounted by the logarithm of its rank.
prompt-injection: Prompt injection is OWASP LLM01: untrusted text manipulates a model into ignoring its instructions. A common escalation is injection into a tool that runs code, turning a text attack into remote code execution. The defense is to give each tool minimal capability rather than trusting the model's input.
excessive-agency: Excessive Agency is OWASP LLM06: an agent does more with a tool or its autonomy than intended. The named mitigations are to limit tool functionality to the minimum needed and to bound autonomy, for example a hard step budget so an agent loop cannot run away and exhaust cost.
idempotency: An idempotent operation produces the same result whether it runs once or many times. HTTP GET, PUT, and DELETE are expected to be idempotent; POST usually is not. Designing writes to be idempotent lets a client safely retry after a timeout without creating duplicates.
database-index: A database index is a sorted structure that lets the engine find rows without scanning the whole table. It speeds up reads that filter or sort on the indexed columns, at the cost of slower writes and extra storage, so you index the columns your queries actually use, not every column.
connection-pool: A connection pool keeps a small set of open database connections and hands them out to requests, because opening a new connection per request is slow and a database caps how many it will accept. Pools also pre-ping and recycle connections so a dropped socket does not surface as a 500.
migration: A schema migration is a versioned, ordered change to a database's structure. Tools like Alembic generate and apply them so the schema evolves reproducibly; the discipline that makes them trustworthy is a test that the migrations and the models produce the same schema, so drift turns CI red.
cross-encoder: A cross-encoder reranker takes a query and a candidate document together and scores their relevance with a model that reads both at once. It is slower than a retriever but more accurate, which is why it runs only over the top candidates a cheaper retriever already surfaced, as a second stage.
token-bucket: A token-bucket rate limiter refills tokens at a fixed rate up to a cap; each request spends one, and a request with no token available is rejected, usually with HTTP 429 and a Retry-After header. It allows short bursts up to the bucket size while bounding the sustained rate.
